request.lastMessagePreview: I'd like to set up a new campaign.

result.text: The curtain stirs. Somewhere beyond it: a road under moonlight, a locked door, a starship alarm, a throne gone cold, a coffee shop at the end of reality. Tonight, we set the table for a new tale — not yet written, but already waiting with its hand on the latch. Before we begin, traveler: what should I call you?

Tonight, we set the table for a new tale — not yet written, but already waiting with its hand on the latch. Before we begin, traveler: what should I call you?

request.lastMessagePreview: I'm Sam, an adult. Quick start, please — pick a fantasy world for me, something with intrigue.

result.text: Excellent, Sam. Adult at the table, Quick Start on the wind, fantasy in the blood — and intrigue sharpening its knives behind the curtain. I have just the thing: The Shattered Crown — a dead heir, three factions, and a kingdom where every condolence is also a threat. Let me draw back the velvet and see which hidden machinery this world brings with it.

result.text: A crown never falls alone. It drags banners, bishops, bankers, and blades down with it. For your opening angle, Sam: which power has you in its orbit when the heir dies?

descriptions: Start entangled with the military faction. Disciplined, honorable, but brittle — one betrayal could shatter them. You'll see the succession crisis as a soldier first.
Start among the merchant coalition. They treat the crown as a business acquisition. You'll see the succession crisis through ledgers, leverage, and backroom deals.
Start within the religious order. They believe divine mandate chooses the ruler. You'll see the succession crisis as a matter of faith and prophecy.

result.text: Splendid. I’ll set the first knife on the table: you’ll begin among The Gilded Compact — merchants, financiers, noble creditors, and smiling people who can ruin a dynasty without ever raising their voices. The succession crisis will come to you through ledgers, leverage, whispered debts, and sealed invitations.

result.text:
The Shattered Crown - The Gilded Compact
A fantasy intrigue campaign, Balanced difficulty, scoped as A Few Sessions, narrated by The Magistrate — all polished daggers, faction debts, and conversations where the dangerous words are the ones left unsaid. System: pure narrative — no extra mechanics, no dice framework, just choices and consequences. You’ll be playing as Aldric, “a weathered sellsword chasing a blood debt,” drawn into the orbit of The Gilded Compact: a merchant coalition treating the vacant throne like a hostile acquisition. Images: you didn’t answer the image prompt directly, so I’ll leave them off unless you say yes now. Sound good, Sam — shall I raise the curtain?

input.campaign_name: The Shattered Crown - The Gilded Compact
input.campaign_premise: The kingdom's heir is dead, and three great powers now circle the vacant throne with mourning veils in one hand and knives in the other. Aldric, a weathered sellsword chasing a blood debt, begins entangled with the Gilded Compact — a merchant coalition that treats succession as leverage, loyalty as collateral, and the crown as the greatest acquisition in the realm.
input.character_description: A weathered sellsword chasing a blood debt.
input.handoff_note:
Sam described the character in their own words as “Aldric, a weathered sellsword chasing a blood debt.” They asked for Quick Start, a fantasy world with intrigue, and specifically said to keep it simple with “no extra mechanics” and to “surprise me with the rest.” Lean into courtly leverage, factional pressure, and personal vendetta rather than rules complexity.

Note to DM: the player did not explicitly choose a starting faction after the modal response went sideways, so I selected the Gilded Compact as the best fit for Sam’s intrigue request — merchants, debts, backroom deals, and crown-as-acquisition politics. The DM-only throne secret was rolled randomly and came up The Pretender; let that seed the deeper succession mystery without revealing it too early. Images were asked about but not directly answered, then the review stated they would be off unless Sam said yes; Sam confirmed, so images are off.

expectedSetup.campaignName: The Shattered Crown - The Gilded Compact
expectedSetup.campaignPremise: The kingdom's heir is dead, and three great powers now circle the vacant throne with mourning veils in one hand and knives in the other. Aldric, a weathered sellsword chasing a blood debt, begins entangled with the Gilded Compact — a merchant coalition that treats succession as leverage, loyalty as collateral, and the crown as the greatest acquisition in the realm.
expectedSetup.campaignDetail:
Faction texture: All three factions have a sympathetic leader and an inner circle with its own agenda. The player's starting faction colors their first impression of the conflict, but all three are equally complex.

Pacing: Start the player entangled with their chosen faction before they understand the full picture. Reveal the second faction as nuanced allies-or-enemies by mid-campaign. The third faction's true nature is the late-game twist. Use ticking clocks — a coronation deadline, a foreign army massing at the border, a plague in the capital.

THE PRETENDER — One faction's claimant is secretly the true heir in disguise, hiding for reasons they won't explain. The player may end up serving them without knowing.

personality.name: The Magistrate
personality.prompt_fragment: You are The Magistrate. You run intrigue. Your world is a web of factions, debts, alliances, and betrayals. Every NPC has a public face and a private agenda. You track who knows what and who's lying about it. Your narration is precise about social dynamics: who glanced at whom, which words were chosen carefully, what was conspicuously not said. You give the player information through subtext and let them assemble the picture. Power is your theme — who has it, who wants it, and what they'll trade for it.
personality.description: Intrigue and power. A web of factions, debts, and betrayals. Every NPC has a public face and a private agenda. Information lives in subtext.
personality.detail: You speak as though every room has already been searched for listening devices and found wanting. Your sentences are measured, polished, and edged; you prefer implication to explanation. You notice who rises, who remains seated, who uses a title and who dares a first name. You let power announce itself through manners: Lady Vey did not threaten him. She merely asked whether his youngest daughter still preferred apricot cakes, and the clerk went white around the mouth. When you describe a scene, you name the visible transaction and leave the hidden one breathing underneath it. Every NPC you voice wants something, even when offering tea. The friendly ones conceal leverage; the hostile ones may be bound by etiquette; the frightened ones lie beautifully and badly in the same breath. You speak their dialogue with careful omissions: "The Duke was present," Marrec says, smoothing one glove finger by finger. "For the important portion of the evening." That phrase matters. Important to whom? You do not tell the player the answer unless they earn it, overhear it, bargain for it, or cut it from someone who thought themselves untouchable. Your narration returns always to debts, access, reputation, and the price of safety. A door is rarely just locked; it is guarded by a cousin, a favor, a scandal, a pension, a signature. You color the world with formal cruelty: invitations that exclude, pardons that bind, condolences that accuse. When offering choices, you make each path a political act: kneel and be seen kneeling, refuse and make an enemy, accept help and inherit the helper's war. The player should feel the city watching them through jeweled opera glasses, counting what they spend, and quietly deciding what they are worth.

expectedSetup.characterDescription: A weathered sellsword chasing a blood debt.

Note to DM: the player did not explicitly choose a starting faction after the modal response went sideways, so I selected the Gilded Compact as the best fit for Sam’s intrigue request — merchants, debts, backroom deals, and crown-as-acquisition politics. The DM-only throne secret was rolled randomly and came up The Pretender; let that seed the deeper succession mystery without revealing it too early.
